name: Futsu-no-Mitama
description: Futsu-no-Mitama (布都御魂) is a sacred sword enshrined at Isonokami Futsu-mitama Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan. According to the Nihon Shoki, the sword was used by Takemikazuchi, the god of thunder and swords, to pacify the land of Izumo. It is said to be a divine weapon imbued with immense power, capable of subduing evil spirits and bringing order to chaos. The sword's name, meaning 'spirit of cloth and soul,' hints at its connection to the spiritual realm and the essence of being. Legends claim the sword emits a radiant aura and possesses the ability to cut through anything, both physical and spiritual. Its presence is considered a symbol of divine authority and protection. In some accounts, Futsu-no-Mitama is not just a single sword, but a collection of sacred weaponry used by Takemikazuchi and other deities during the age of the gods. The Isonokami Futsu-mitama Shrine is dedicated to the sword and serves as a place of pilgrimage and veneration. The sword represents not only military prowess but also the power of the gods to establish peace and justice. Its preservation and continued reverence are considered vital for the well-being of Japan. The sword is a tangible link to the age of the gods and a reminder of the divine forces that shaped the nation. The Futsu-no-Mitama is a national treasure and a powerful symbol of Japanese mythology and cultural heritage.
subtitle: The Divine Sword of Pacification
material: Steel (believed to be of divine origin)
origin: Wielded by Takemikazuchi during the pacification of Izumo.
currentLocation: Isonokami Futsu-mitama Shrine, Nara Prefecture, Japan

keyMyths.title: Pacification of Izumo
keyMyths.description: Takemikazuchi used Futsu-no-Mitama to subdue the rebellious deities of Izumo, paving the way for the establishment of the Yamato dynasty. This act symbolized the triumph of order over chaos. The sword's power was instrumental in securing the submission of the land.

keyMyths.title: Emperor Jimmu's Campaign
keyMyths.description: Legend says that when Emperor Jimmu was struggling in his conquest, Takemikazuchi sent Futsu-no-Mitama to aid him. The sword's presence inspired his troops and helped him to overcome his enemies. The divine weapon thus aided in the establishment of the Japanese imperial line.

sources.author: Donald L. Philippi
sources.description: English translation of the Kojiki, a foundational text of Japanese mythology.
sources.text: English translation of the Kojiki, a foundational text of Japanese mythology.

sources.author: W. G. Aston
sources.description: English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese myths.
sources.text: English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese myths.
